docs.title: TaskMQ Documentation
docs.text: TaskMQ is a modern, developer-friendly Python task queue and job processing framework for background jobs, automation, and scalable systems.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Simple CLI and REST API for job management
  • Decorator-based custom handler registration
  • Pluggable storage backends (SQLite, Redis)
  • Configurable retry policies and job scheduling
  • JWT authentication and Prometheus metrics
  • Full async handler support

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: Guide Description Quick Start Get up and running in 5 minutes Usage Guide Detailed CLI and library usage Writing Handlers Create custom job handlers API Reference REST API endpoints and authentication Contributing Development setup and guidelines

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install task-mq

# With Redis support
pip install task-mq[redis]

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
from taskmq.jobs.handlers import register_handler
from taskmq.worker import Worker
from taskmq.storage.sqlite_backend import SQLiteBackend

@register_handler("greet")
def greet_handler(job):
    name = job.payload.get("name", "World")
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

backend = SQLiteBackend()
backend.insert_job('{"name": "TaskMQ"}', handler="greet")

worker = Worker(max_workers=1, backend=backend)
worker.start()

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
• GitHub Repository
  • PyPI Package
  • Issue Tracker

Author: Varun Gupta

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: TaskMQ provides a REST API for job management, built with FastAPI.

docs.title: Starting the API Server
docs.text:
taskmq serve-api

The server starts at http://127.0.0.1:8000. Interactive documentation is available at:

  • Swagger UI: http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs
  • ReDoc: http://127.0.0.1:8000/redoc

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: All endpoints (except /health and /monitor/metrics) require JWT authentication.

docs.title: Getting a Token
docs.text:
Generate a JWT token with your secret key:

import jwt
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, UTC

secret = "your-secret-key"  # Same as TASKMQ_JWT_SECRET env var
payload = {
    "sub": "admin",
    "role": "admin",
    "exp": datetime.now(UTC) + timedelta(hours=24)
}
token = jwt.encode(payload, secret, algorithm="HS256")
print(token)

docs.title: Using the Token
docs.text:
Include the token in the Authorization header:

curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8000/add-job \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <your-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"payload": {"task": "test"}, "handler": "dummy"}'

docs.title: Roles
docs.text: Role Permissions admin All operations (add, cancel, retry jobs) worker Retry jobs only

docs.title: Health Check
docs.text:
Check if the API and database are healthy.

GET /health

Response:

{
  "status": "ok"
}

No authentication required.

docs.title: Add Job
docs.text:
Add a new job to the queue.

POST /add-job

Request Body:

{
  "payload": {"key": "value"},
  "handler": "handler_name",
  "priority": 10,
  "retry_policy": "fixed",
  "scheduled_for": "2026-04-01T12:00:00Z",
  "interval_seconds": null
}
Field Type Required Description payload object Yes Job data handler string Yes Handler name priority int No 0 (Low), 10 (Normal), 20 (High). Default: 0 retry_policy string No fixed, exponential, or none. Default: fixed scheduled_for string No ISO 8601 datetime for delayed execution interval_seconds int No Repeat interval for periodic jobs

Response:

{
  "status": "ok",
  "job_id": 123
}

Auth: admin

docs.title: Cancel Job
docs.text:
Cancel a pending job.

POST /cancel

Request Body:

{
  "job_id": 123
}

Response:

{
  "status": "cancelled",
  "job_id": 123
}

Auth: admin

Note: Only pending jobs can be cancelled. Running jobs complete normally.

docs.title: Retry Job
docs.text:
Retry a failed job.

POST /retry

Request Body:

{
  "job_id": 123
}

Response:

{
  "status": "retrying",
  "job_id": 123
}

Auth: admin, worker

docs.title: Metrics
docs.text:
Prometheus-formatted metrics for monitoring.

GET /monitor/metrics

Response:

# HELP taskmq_jobs_total Total number of jobs processed
# TYPE taskmq_jobs_total counter
taskmq_jobs_total{status="success"} 150
taskmq_jobs_total{status="failed"} 5

# HELP taskmq_job_duration_seconds Job processing duration
# TYPE taskmq_job_duration_seconds histogram
taskmq_job_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.1"} 50
...

No authentication required.

docs.title: Using httpx
docs.text:
import httpx

BASE_URL = "http://127.0.0.1:8000"
TOKEN = "your-jwt-token"
HEADERS = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {TOKEN}"}

# Add a job
response = httpx.post(
    f"{BASE_URL}/add-job",
    json={"payload": {"task": "process"}, "handler": "mytask"},
    headers=HEADERS
)
job_id = response.json()["job_id"]
print(f"Created job: {job_id}")

# Cancel a job
response = httpx.post(
    f"{BASE_URL}/cancel",
    json={"job_id": job_id},
    headers=HEADERS
)
print(response.json())

docs.title: Using requests
docs.text:
import requests

BASE_URL = "http://127.0.0.1:8000"
TOKEN = "your-jwt-token"
HEADERS = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {TOKEN}"}

# Add a scheduled job
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, UTC

future = (datetime.now(UTC) + timedelta(hours=1)).isoformat()
response = requests.post(
    f"{BASE_URL}/add-job",
    json={
        "payload": {"scheduled": True},
        "handler": "mytask",
        "scheduled_for": future
    },
    headers=HEADERS
)
print(response.json())

docs.title: Async Client
docs.text:
import asyncio
import httpx

async def add_jobs():
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        headers = {"Authorization": "Bearer your-token"}

        tasks = []
        for i in range(10):
            task = client.post(
                "http://127.0.0.1:8000/add-job",
                json={"payload": {"index": i}, "handler": "batch"},
                headers=headers
            )
            tasks.append(task)

        responses = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
        for r in responses:
            print(r.json())

asyncio.run(add_jobs())

docs.title: 401 Unauthorized

{
  "detail": "Invalid or missing token"
}

docs.title: 403 Forbidden

docs.title: 422 Validation Error
docs.text:
{
  "detail": [
    {
      "loc": ["body", "handler"],
      "msg": "field required",
      "type": "value_error.missing"
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: Variable Description Required TASKMQ_JWT_SECRET Secret key for JWT signing Yes TASKMQ_BACKEND Storage backend (sqlite or redis) No TASKMQ_REDIS_URL Redis connection URL If using Redis

docs.title: Example Setup
docs.text:
export TASKMQ_JWT_SECRET="your-secure-secret-key"
export TASKMQ_BACKEND="redis"
export TASKMQ_REDIS_URL="redis://localhost:6379/0"

taskmq serve-api

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to TaskMQ! This guide will help you get started.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.8 or higher
  • Git
  • Redis (optional, for running Redis tests)

docs.title: Clone and Install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/gvarun01/task-mq.git
cd task-mq

# Create virtual environment
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate  # On Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate

# Install with development dependencies
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
# Run tests
pytest -v

# Run linting
ruff check taskmq tests

# Run type checking
mypy taskmq

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
task-mq/
├── taskmq/                 # Main package
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── cli.py              # CLI commands
│   ├── api_server.py       # FastAPI application
│   ├── worker.py           # Job processing worker
│   ├── jobs/
│   │   └── handlers.py     # Handler registry
│   └── storage/
│       ├── base.py         # Abstract backend interface
│       ├── sqlite_backend.py
│       └── redis_backend.py
├── tests/                  # Test suite
├── docs/                   # Documentation
├── pyproject.toml          # Project configuration
└── README.md

docs.title: All Tests
docs.text: pytest -v

docs.title: Specific Test File
docs.text: pytest tests/test_worker.py -v

docs.title: With Coverage
docs.text:
pytest --cov=taskmq --cov-report=html
# Open htmlcov/index.html in browser

docs.title: Redis Tests
docs.text:
Redis tests require a running Redis instance:

# Start Redis (Docker)
docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis:latest

# Run all tests including Redis
TASKMQ_REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6379/0 pytest -v

docs.title: Code Style
docs.text: TaskMQ uses Ruff for linting and formatting.

docs.title: Check for Issues
docs.text: ruff check taskmq tests

docs.title: Auto-fix Issues
docs.text: ruff check --fix taskmq tests

docs.title: Format Code
docs.text: ruff format taskmq tests

docs.title: Type Checking
docs.text: mypy taskmq

docs.title: Style Guidelines
docs.text:
• Follow PEP 8 conventions
  • Use type hints for function signatures
  • Write Google-style docstrings for public functions
  • Keep functions focused and small
  • Prefer explicit over implicit

docs.title: Example Function
docs.text:
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Optional


def process_item(
    item_id: int,
    options: Optional[dict] = None
) -> dict:
    """Process a single item with the given options.

    Args:
        item_id: The unique identifier for the item.
        options: Optional processing options.

    Returns:
        A dict containing the processing result with keys:
        - 'status': Either 'success' or 'failed'
        - 'item_id': The processed item ID

    Raises:
        ValueError: If item_id is negative.
    """
    if item_id < 0:
        raise ValueError("item_id must be non-negative")

    options = options or {}
    # Processing logic here
    return {"status": "success", "item_id": item_id}

docs.title: Test Structure
docs.text:
import pytest
from taskmq.storage.base import Job, JobStatus


class TestWorker:
    """Tests for the Worker class."""

    def test_processes_job_successfully(self, sqlite_backend):
        """Worker should process a job and update its status."""
        # Arrange
        job_id = sqlite_backend.insert_job('{"test": true}', handler="dummy")

        # Act
        # ... run worker ...

        # Assert
        job = sqlite_backend.get_job(job_id)
        assert job.status == JobStatus.SUCCESS

docs.title: Using Fixtures
docs.text:
TaskMQ provides test fixtures in tests/conftest.py:

  • sqlite_backend - Isolated SQLite backend (cleaned up after test)
  • redis_backend - Isolated Redis backend (skipped if Redis unavailable)
  • backend - Parametrized fixture that runs tests with both backends

docs.title: Before Submitting
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository and create a feature branch

  2. Write tests for new functionality or bug fixes

  3. Run the test suite and ensure all tests pass: 

    pytest -v

  4. Run linting and type checking: 

    ruff check taskmq tests
mypy taskmq

  5. Update documentation if needed

docs.title: Submitting
docs.text:
1. Push your branch to your fork

  2. Open a pull request against main

  3. Fill out the PR template with:

  4. Description of changes
  5. Related issue (if any)

  6. Testing performed

  7. Wait for CI checks to pass

  8. Address review feedback

docs.title: Commit Messages
docs.text:
Follow conventional commit format:

type: short description

Longer explanation if needed.

Fixes #123

Types: - feat: New feature - fix: Bug fix - docs: Documentation changes - test: Test additions or fixes - refactor: Code refactoring - chore: Build/tooling changes

docs.title: Building Documentation
docs.text:
Documentation is built with MkDocs:

# Install docs dependencies
pip install -e ".[docs]"

# Serve locally (with hot reload)
mkdocs serve

# Build static site
mkdocs build

docs.title: Reporting Issues
docs.text:
When reporting bugs, please include:

  1. Python version (python --version)
  2. TaskMQ version (pip show task-mq)
  3. Operating system
  4. Steps to reproduce
  5. Expected vs actual behavior
  6. Error messages or logs

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• GitHub Issues - Bug reports and feature requests
  • Discussions - Questions and general discussion

docs.title: License
docs.text: By contributing, you agree that your contributions will be licensed under the MIT License.

docs.title: Writing Handlers
docs.text: Handlers are Python functions that process jobs. This guide covers handler registration, patterns, and best practices.

docs.title: Basic Handler
docs.text:
Register a handler using the @register_handler decorator:

from taskmq.jobs.handlers import register_handler

@register_handler("send_email")
def send_email(job):
    """Send an email based on job payload.

    Args:
        job: Job object with id, payload, status, and other attributes.

    Returns:
        A result dict that will be stored with the job.
    """
    payload = job.payload
    to = payload.get("to")
    subject = payload.get("subject")
    body = payload.get("body")

    # Your email sending logic here
    print(f"Sending email to {to}: {subject}")

    return {"status": "sent", "recipient": to}

docs.title: Job Object
docs.text:
The job parameter provides access to job data:

Attribute Type Description job.id int Unique job identifier job.payload dict/str Job data (parsed JSON or string) job.status JobStatus Current status (PENDING, RUNNING, etc.) job.handler str Handler name job.priority int Priority level (0, 10, 20) job.retries int Number of retry attempts job.created_at datetime When the job was created job.result str Result from previous execution (if any) job.error_log str Error message (if failed)

docs.title: Async Handlers
docs.text:
TaskMQ supports async handlers natively:

import asyncio
import httpx
from taskmq.jobs.handlers import register_handler

@register_handler("fetch_data")
async def fetch_data(job):
    """Fetch data from an external API."""
    url = job.payload.get("url")

    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        response = await client.get(url)
        data = response.json()

    return {"fetched": True, "data_size": len(data)}

docs.title: Handler Return Values
docs.text:
Handlers can return various types:

@register_handler("example")
def example_handler(job):
    # Return a dict (recommended)
    return {"status": "success", "count": 42}

    # Return a string
    return "Task completed"

    # Return None (no result stored)
    return None

The return value is converted to a string and stored in job.result.

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Unhandled exceptions trigger the retry policy:

@register_handler("risky_task")
def risky_task(job):
    """Task that might fail."""
    if some_condition:
        raise ValueError("Something went wrong")

    return {"status": "ok"}

For controlled failures without retry:

@register_handler("validate")
def validate(job):
    """Validate data, fail fast on invalid input."""
    data = job.payload

    if not data.get("required_field"):
        # Log the error but don't raise (won't trigger retry)
        return {"status": "failed", "reason": "missing required_field"}

    return {"status": "valid"}

docs.title: Processing with External Services
docs.text:
import httpx
from taskmq.jobs.handlers import register_handler

@register_handler("webhook")
def send_webhook(job):
    """Send a webhook notification."""
    url = job.payload.get("url")
    data = job.payload.get("data")

    response = httpx.post(url, json=data, timeout=30)
    response.raise_for_status()

    return {
        "status_code": response.status_code,
        "delivered": True
    }

docs.title: Batch Processing
docs.text:
@register_handler("batch_process")
def batch_process(job):
    """Process multiple items in a single job."""
    items = job.payload.get("items", [])
    results = []

    for item in items:
        result = process_item(item)
        results.append(result)

    return {
        "processed": len(results),
        "results": results
    }

docs.title: Chaining Jobs
docs.text:
from taskmq.storage import get_backend

@register_handler("step1")
def step1(job):
    """First step, creates next job."""
    result = do_step1(job.payload)

    # Queue the next step
    backend = get_backend()
    backend.insert_job(
        payload=result,
        handler="step2"
    )

    return {"step": 1, "next_queued": True}

@register_handler("step2")
def step2(job):
    """Second step."""
    return {"step": 2, "complete": True}

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Keep handlers focused - One handler, one responsibility

  2. Make handlers idempotent - Safe to run multiple times with same input

  3. Handle timeouts - Set appropriate timeouts for external calls

  4. Return meaningful results - Include status and relevant data

  5. Use structured payloads - Prefer dicts over strings for payload

  6. Log important events - Use Python logging for debugging

import logging
from taskmq.jobs.handlers import register_handler

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@register_handler("important_task")
def important_task(job):
    logger.info(f"Starting job {job.id}")

    try:
        result = do_work(job.payload)
        logger.info(f"Job {job.id} completed successfully")
        return result
    except Exception as e:
        logger.error(f"Job {job.id} failed: {e}")
        raise

docs.title: Handler Discovery
docs.text:
Handlers must be imported before workers start. Common patterns:

Option 1: Import in worker script

# worker.py
import myapp.handlers  # Registers all handlers
from taskmq.worker import Worker

Worker().start()

Option 2: Package init.py

# myapp/handlers/__init__.py
from .email import send_email
from .sms import send_sms
from .webhooks import send_webhook

Option 3: Entry point registration (advanced)

Define handlers as package entry points for automatic discovery.

docs.title: Testing Handlers
docs.text:
import pytest
from taskmq.storage.base import Job, JobStatus
from myapp.handlers import send_email

def test_send_email():
    """Test email handler."""
    job = Job(
        id=1,
        payload={"to": "test@example.com", "subject": "Test"},
        status=JobStatus.RUNNING
    )

    result = send_email(job)

    assert result["status"] == "sent"
    assert result["recipient"] == "test@example.com"

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• API Reference - REST API documentation
  • Usage Guide - CLI and configuration options

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get TaskMQ running in under 5 minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.8 or higher
  • pip package manager

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install task-mq

For Redis support:

pip install task-mq[redis]

docs.title: Step 1: Start a Worker
docs.text:
Open a terminal and start a worker process:

taskmq run-worker --max-workers 1

You should see:

Starting worker pool with 1 worker(s) using sqlite backend... Press Ctrl+C to stop.

docs.title: Step 2: Add a Job
docs.text:
Open another terminal and add a job:

taskmq add-job --payload '{"message": "Hello, World!"}' --handler dummy

Output:

Inserted job with ID: 1, handler: dummy, priority: 0, payload: {'message': 'Hello, World!'}

docs.title: Step 3: Check Job Status
docs.text:
taskmq get-job 1

Output:

Job ID: 1
Status: success
Priority: 0
Payload: {"message": "Hello, World!"}
Result: {'executed': True, 'job_id': 1}
Retries: 0

docs.title: Step 4: Start the API Server
docs.text:
taskmq serve-api

Visit http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs for interactive API documentation.

docs.title: Using Redis Backend
docs.text:
For production workloads, use Redis:

# Start worker with Redis
taskmq --backend redis --redis-url redis://localhost:6379/0 run-worker

# Add job with Redis
taskmq --backend redis --redis-url redis://localhost:6379/0 add-job --payload '{"task": "test"}' --handler dummy

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
pip install task-mq[dev]
pytest -v

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Usage Guide - Learn about all CLI commands and options
  • Writing Handlers - Create custom job handlers
  • API Reference - Use the REST API

docs.title: Usage Guide
docs.text: Complete guide to using TaskMQ via CLI and Python.

docs.title: Running Workers
docs.text:
Start worker processes to consume jobs from the queue:

# Single worker (default)
taskmq run-worker

# Multiple workers
taskmq run-worker --max-workers 4

# With Redis backend
taskmq --backend redis --redis-url redis://localhost:6379/0 run-worker

Workers automatically handle graceful shutdown on Ctrl+C (SIGINT) or SIGTERM.

docs.title: Managing Jobs
docs.text:
Add a job:

taskmq add-job --payload '{"data": "value"}' --handler mytask

# With priority (0=Low, 10=Normal, 20=High)
taskmq add-job --payload '{"urgent": true}' --handler mytask --priority 20

Get job details:

taskmq get-job 123

Inspect job timeline:

taskmq inspect 123

Search job logs:

# By job ID
taskmq logs --job 123

# By handler name
taskmq logs --handler mytask --limit 100

docs.title: Dead Letter Queue
docs.text:
Jobs that fail all retry attempts are moved to the DLQ:

# List dead jobs
taskmq list-dead --limit 20

# Replay a dead job (resets retry count)
taskmq replay-dead 123

docs.title: Job Replay
docs.text:
Replay any job to re-run with the same parameters:

# Basic replay
taskmq replay 123

# Exact replay (fails if handler code changed)
taskmq replay 123 --exact

docs.title: API Server
docs.text:
taskmq serve-api

The API server runs on http://127.0.0.1:8000 by default.

docs.title: Basic Example
docs.text:
from taskmq.jobs.handlers import register_handler
from taskmq.worker import Worker
from taskmq.storage.sqlite_backend import SQLiteBackend

# Register a handler
@register_handler("process_data")
def process_data(job):
    """Process incoming data."""
    data = job.payload
    result = {"processed": True, "input": data}
    return result

# Create backend and add job
backend = SQLiteBackend()
job_id = backend.insert_job(
    payload='{"values": [1, 2, 3]}',
    handler="process_data"
)

# Start worker
worker = Worker(max_workers=2, backend=backend)
worker.start()  # Blocks until stopped

docs.title: Using Redis Backend
docs.text:
from taskmq.storage.redis_backend import RedisBackend

backend = RedisBackend(redis_url="redis://localhost:6379/0")
job_id = backend.insert_job(payload, handler="mytask")

docs.title: Job Options
docs.text:
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, UTC

# With retry policy
backend.insert_job(
    payload='{"data": "test"}',
    handler="mytask",
    retry_policy="exponential"  # or "fixed", "none"
)

# With priority
backend.insert_job(
    payload='{"urgent": true}',
    handler="mytask",
    priority=20  # High priority
)

# Scheduled for future
future_time = datetime.now(UTC) + timedelta(hours=1)
backend.insert_job(
    payload='{"scheduled": true}',
    handler="mytask",
    scheduled_for=future_time
)

# Periodic job
backend.insert_job(
    payload='{"recurring": true}',
    handler="cleanup",
    interval_seconds=3600  # Every hour
)

docs.title: Worker Configuration
docs.text:
worker = Worker(
    max_workers=4,          # Thread pool size
    backend=backend,        # Storage backend
    poll_interval=1.0,      # Seconds between queue checks
    max_retries=3,          # Max retry attempts
    lock_timeout=30         # Job lock timeout in seconds
)

docs.title: Handler Registration
docs.text:
Handlers must be registered before workers start. There are two approaches:

1. Same module (simple scripts):

from taskmq.jobs.handlers import register_handler
from taskmq.worker import Worker

@register_handler("mytask")
def my_handler(job):
    return {"done": True}

worker = Worker()
worker.start()

2. Separate module (recommended for larger projects):

# handlers.py
from taskmq.jobs.handlers import register_handler

@register_handler("email")
def send_email(job):
    # email logic
    pass

@register_handler("sms")
def send_sms(job):
    # sms logic
    pass

# worker.py
import handlers  # Import to register handlers
from taskmq.worker import Worker

worker = Worker()
worker.start()

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text: Variable Description Default TASKMQ_BACKEND Storage backend (sqlite or redis) sqlite TASKMQ_REDIS_URL Redis connection URL redis://localhost:6379/0 TASKMQ_JWT_SECRET JWT signing secret for API auth (required for API)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Writing Handlers - Advanced handler patterns
  • API Reference - REST API documentation
